report:
## Your Health Assessment

Hi Jessie, thanks for taking the time to complete this. Your answers paint a clear and interconnected picture, and the good news is that the patterns we're seeing tend to respond well when addressed at their roots.

### Your Anxiety Has a Body, Not Just a Mind

The hypervigilance, racing heart, difficulty breathing, and muscle tension you described are not just "mental" symptoms. They are the physical signature of an overactive stress response: elevated adrenaline and cortisol driving your nervous system into a sustained fight-or-flight state. The fact that your mind races at bedtime fits perfectly here. These same stress hormones that keep you on edge during the day make it very hard for the brain to quiet down at night.

What's striking is how your eating pattern may be fueling this cycle. Skipping breakfast after an overnight fast keeps cortisol elevated well into the day, since your body produces stress hormones to maintain blood sugar when food isn't coming in. Research consistently links [skipping breakfast to elevated cortisol, anxiety, and disrupted circadian signaling](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0271531725000946). Your morning coffee without food can amplify this further. In other words, part of your anxiety may literally be your body screaming for fuel.

### A Gut That's Quietly Running the Show

Your bloating and constipation are not isolated digestive complaints. They point to gut dysbiosis and slowed motility, both of which have far-reaching effects. [Disruptions in gut microbial balance trigger neuroinflammation, oxidative stress, and neurotransmitter imbalances](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiomes/articles/10.3389/frmbi.2025.1701608/full) that are closely tied to anxiety and poor mental clarity. The gut produces the majority of the body's serotonin, and when it's irritated, excess serotonin can actually promote anxiety, disrupt sleep quality, and suppress motivation, the very things you're struggling with.

Your reliance on fast food and frozen meals also means a high intake of seed oils and food additives, both of which directly damage the gut lining, feed pathogenic bacteria, and drive the inflammation that keeps this cycle going.

### The Cold Hands Tell a Metabolic Story

Frequently cold extremities are one of the most reliable signs of low thyroid function. Thyroid hormone is the master controller of your metabolic rate, and when it's insufficient, energy production slows across every system. This connects directly to your moderate energy levels, your constipation and bloating ([hypothyroidism slows gut motility, leading to gas buildup and sluggish digestion](https://www.mdpi.com/2039-4403/15/10/354)), and even your difficulty with sleep and mental clarity. Low thyroid function also makes you more susceptible to stress, creating a reinforcing loop with the anxiety pattern above.

### The Through Line

Your symptoms are not a random collection of unrelated issues. They form a coherent loop: a diet pattern that elevates stress hormones, a gut under strain from both stress and dietary irritants, and a metabolism that may not be producing enough energy to support any of these systems properly. The anxiety, the bloating, the constipation, the cold hands, the broken sleep, and the foggy energy all trace back to the same interconnected roots.

---

This quiz gives us a meaningful starting point, but it's just that. There's more to explore, especially around your metabolic and gut health, to build a complete picture of what's driving these patterns for you specifically.
